revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/84413436|形代]]"
revision.text:
'''形代'''（かたしろ）とは、[[Soul|神霊]]が依り憑く（よりつく）[[Yorishiro|依り代]]の一種。人間の霊を宿す場合は人形を用いるなど、神霊が依り憑き易いように形を整えた物を指す。身代わり信仰により、人間の身代わりとされた。[[March|3月]]の[[Double Third Festival|上巳]]の節句に、この形代で体の調子の悪いところを撫でて（このようなものを撫物という）穢れを遷した後に川や海に流し、[[Child|子供]]の成育を祈ることがあった{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 歴史 == [[ファイル:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|平安京]]出土 人形代

{{Small|[[京都市考古資料館]]展示。}}]] 形代は[[Ancient history|古代]]から存在したとされ、[[Jōmon period|縄文時代]]の[[Dogū|土偶]]、[[Yayoi period|弥生時代]]の人面土器、[[Kofun period|古墳時代]]の人物埴輪などにも確認された。水に流すものであることから、草木などが素材として使用された。[[Japan|日本]]では古代から現在に至るまで、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』や『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』などにも記されている「大祓」という行事が全国の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]で開かれている{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. 一部に人形代や短冊、笹竹を焚き上げるということが行われるが、これはどんと焼きや[[Vajrayana|密教]]に由来する行事であり[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|神仏習合]]で混用されたと考えられる。 現在の「流し雛」や「七夕雛」などは、形代の名残だとされる{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

A [[Yorishiro]] is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of. In the belief of [[substitute]]s, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in [[March]], people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 歴史 == [[ファイル:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|平安京]]出土 人形代

{{Small|[[京都市考古資料館]]展示。}}]] 形代は[[Ancient history|古代]]から存在したとされ、[[Jōmon period|縄文時代]]の[[Dogū|土偶]]、[[Yayoi period|弥生時代]]の人面土器、[[Kofun period|古墳時代]]の人物埴輪などにも確認された。水に流すものであることから、草木などが素材として使用された。[[Japan|日本]]では古代から現在に至るまで、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』や『[[Engishiki|延喜式]]』などにも記されている「大祓」という行事が全国の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]で開かれている{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. 一部に人形代や短冊、笹竹を焚き上げるということが行われるが、これはどんと焼きや[[Vajrayana|密教]]に由来する行事であり[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|神仏習合]]で混用されたと考えられる。 現在の「流し雛」や「七夕雛」などは、形代の名残だとされる{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

A [[Yorishiro]] is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of. In the belief of [[substitute]]s, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in [[March]], people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 歴史 == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|平安京]]出土 人形代

This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. 一部に人形代や短冊、笹竹を焚き上げるということが行われるが、これはどんと焼きや[[Vajrayana|密教]]に由来する行事であり[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|神仏習合]]で混用されたと考えられる。 現在の「流し雛」や「七夕雛」などは、形代の名残だとされる{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

A [[Yorishiro]] is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of. In the belief of [[substitute]]s, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in [[March]], people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 歴史 == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|平安京]]出土 人形代

[[Kyoto City Archaological Museum]]]] Katajiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. 一部に人形代や短冊、笹竹を焚き上げるということが行われるが、これはどんと焼きや[[Vajrayana|密教]]に由来する行事であり[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|神仏習合]]で混用されたと考えられる。 現在の「流し雛」や「七夕雛」などは、形代の名残だとされる{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

A [[Yorishiro]] is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of. In the belief of [[substitute]]s, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in [[March]], people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 歴史 == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|平安京]]出土 人形代

[[Kyoto City Archaological Museum]]]] Katajiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. Today's "Nagashi-bina" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

In the belief of [[substitute]]s, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in [[March]], people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|Heian-kyo]] unearthed humanoid generation

[[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katajiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. Today's "Nagashi-bina" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020年10月27日|publisher=}}。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * 流し雛 * 大祓（6月の名越祓で形代を使う） * 天児（あまがつ） * [[Hōko (doll)|這子]]（ほうこ） * [[Yorishiro|尸童]]（よりまし） * 紙雛 * [[Hinamatsuri|雛人形]] * [[Straw dog|藁人形]] * [[Amulet|魔除け]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. Today's "Nagashi-bina" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.。 == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220219163324|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:形代]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]'' 22:08, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}} A [[Yorishiro]] is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of. In the belief of [[substitute]]s{{Disambiguation needed|date=February 2022}}, they were used as substitutes for humans. During the [[Double Third Festival]] in March, people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=}}。 == History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|Heian-kyo]] unearthed humanoid generation

[[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katajiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. Today's "Nagashi-bina" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.。 == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220219163324|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:形代]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]'' 22:08, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}} A {{ill|Katashiro|ja|形代}} is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}. In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=}}。 == History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|Heian-kyo]] unearthed humanoid generation

This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. ==Remnants == Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.。 == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220226061811|u=Immanuelle|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220219163324|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:形代]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]'' 22:08, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}} A {{ill|Katashiro|ja|形代}} is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge in. It is a kind of [[Yorishiro]] that [[Soul|Divine Spirit]] takes possession of{{Citation needed|date=February 2022}}. In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people used these dolls to stroke the parts of their bodies that were not in good shape (this kind of thing is called furoimono), and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[child]]s.{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=}}{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018}}。 == History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|Heian-kyo]] unearthed humanoid generation

[[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.。In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. ==Remnants == Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.。 == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan. In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}} ==Remnants == Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo. == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Shinto religious objects]]

revision.comment: slight misunderstanding on my part: nademono refers not to this usage of the dolls, but to the dolls themselves insofar as they are used this way.

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} A {{nihongo||形代|'''Katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[Yorishiro]]}} in which a [[Soul|Godly Spirit]] takes refuge. In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}} During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}} == History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|[[Heian-kyō|Heian-kyo]] unearthed humanoid generation

[[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan. In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo bamboos is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}} ==Remnants == Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo. == See also == * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]] == References == == External links == * [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形 [[Category:Shinto religious objects]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}}
A {{nihongo||形代|'''Katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[Yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name=":0"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.<ref name=":0"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''Katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[Yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name=":0"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katayo.<ref name=":0"/>

Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

[[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Dolls]] [[Category:Dd]] {{Shinto shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==
* [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形

The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] to [[Category:kk]][[en:{{subst:FULLPAGENAME}}]][[Category:kk]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==

The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

[[en:Katashiro]][[en:Katashiro]]{{Shinto shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

== External links ==

[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Dolls]] [[en:Katashiro]][[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[en:Katashiro]][[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[en:Katashiro]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Katashiro]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Katashiro]][[en:Katashiro]]{{Shinto shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==

This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
