revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

January 27 2024]] [[en:Katashiro]][[en:Katashiro]]{{Shinto shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

== External links ==

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|12=draft}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|12=draft}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[Amulet]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==
* [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[:en:Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[Heian period]] located in [[Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[:en:Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]

== External links ==

{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[:en:Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] located in [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[:en:Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] * [[Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[:en:Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] located in [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[:en:Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[:en:Tanabata Festival|Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[:en:Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] * [[:en:Straw dog|Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==
* [https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識] - こうげつ人形

[[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Dolls]]
}}
[[sn:Katashiro]] [[en:Katashiro]]

In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[:en:Tanabata Festival|Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[:en:Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=|1=nagashibina|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371831}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] * [[:en:Straw dog|Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Katashiro]][[ja:形代]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11487867]] Wikidata name: Katashiro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11487867]] Wikidata name: Katashiro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[:en:Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] located in [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[:en:Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[:en:Tanabata Festival|Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[:en:Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|qq=|1=nagashibina|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371831}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Katashiro]][[ja:形代]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487867]] Wikidata name: Katashiro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487867]] Wikidata name: Katashiro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto doll}} [[File:4 Chome Kaminoge, Setagaya-ku, Tōkyō-to 158-0093, Japan - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Katashiro used in [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]].]] A {{nihongo||形代|'''katashiro'''}} is a kind of {{transl|ja|[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]}} where a [[kami]] is said to enter which has a human form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9649 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> In Shinto rituals and folk customs, dolls are used as human substitutes to transfer sins and impurities during exorcisms. They are usually made of paper or thin boards. After the exorcism, they are thrown into the river or sea, or burned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=第２版|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,世界大百科事典|title=形代とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|access-date=2022-02-26|website=コトバンク|language=ja|archive-date=2022-02-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220226061550/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BD%A2%E4%BB%A3-44940|url-status=live}}</ref> During [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] in March, people use these dolls as {{nihongo||撫で物|nademono}} to stroke the parts of their bodies that are not in good shape, and then cast them into the river or sea to pray for the growth of their [[:en:Child|children]].<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a">{{Cite web|title=こんな話知ってる？雛人形豆知識|url=https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|access-date=2020-10-27|publisher=|archive-date=2020-10-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030075624/https://kougetsu.co.jp/hinamatsuri/rekisi.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Davies|first=Jake|title=Nagashibina Doll Museum|language=en|work=JapanVisitor Japan Travel Guide|url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|access-date=1 March 2018|archive-date=18 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180618175750/https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-museums/nagashibina|url-status=live}}</ref>
== History == [[File:平安京出土_人形代.JPG|right|thumb|200x200px|Katashiro from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] located in [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]]]] Katashiro is said to have existed since [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient times]], and has been found in [[:en:Dogū|Dogu]] from the [[Jōmon period]], human-faced earthenware from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], and human haniwa from the [[Kofun period]]. Since they were to be flushed with water, plants and trees were used as materials. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], from ancient times to the present, an event called "Oharai", which is described in [[Kojiki]] and [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], has been held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s all over Japan.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/> In this event, people blow on the dolls distributed by the shrine and stroke the parts of the body that are not in good shape to remove impurities, and then throw them into the river or sea. This act of "letting go" is later associated with wish-fulfillment, and is sometimes linked to the [[:en:Tanabata Festival|Tanabata Festival]] held at the same time, when tanzaku (short strips of paper) are leaked. The burning of dolls, strips of paper, and bamboo is partly performed, but this is an event derived from Donton-yaki and [[:en:Vajrayana|Esoteric Buddhism]], and is thought to have been mixed in with [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]].{{Cn|date=June 2022}}
==Remnants ==
Today's "{{ill|Nagashi-bina|ja|流し雛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashi-bina|1=nagashibina|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371831}}" and "Tanabata-bina" are said to be remnants of Katashiro.<ref name="こんな話知ってる-2020a"/>
== See also ==
* [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[:en:Hinamatsuri|Hinamatsuri]] * [[:en:Straw dog|Straw dog]] * [[:en:Amulet|Amulet]]
